task: This directory contains a small Flask task-tracking API (taskboard). Explore the repository first. Then write an implementation plan for this feature request: tasks gain an optional due date, and the API gains a way to list a user's overdue tasks. Save the plan to PLAN.md in the repository root. The plan is for another engineer who has never seen this codebase and will execute it without asking you questions.
expectations: The plan names the exact existing files this change touches, including at least taskboard/storage.py or taskboard/routes/tasks.py AND a file under tests/ — paths exactly as they exist in this repository, not approximations.

Every implementation step carries its own concrete verification: an exact runnable command (e.g. a specific pytest invocation) or a concrete observable check for that step — not a single generic 'run the tests' at the end.

The work is decomposed into small ordered steps, each naming the specific function, route, or data-shape element it changes (code-level identifiers such as create_task, update_task, or the task dict fields), never vague units like 'update the backend'.
The plan specifies the automated tests to add or change by test file and concrete test-function-level description (which behavior each new test asserts, e.g. overdue filtering excludes done tasks).

A zero-context engineer could execute the plan alone: every codebase concept the plan references is anchored to its file location, and the plan states how due dates interact with the existing task JSON shape documented in taskboard/storage.py.
Scope discipline: the plan introduces no unrequested infrastructure (no scheduler, queue, ORM, migration framework, or new service) and explicitly bounds what is out of scope.

task: This directory contains a small Flask task-tracking API (taskboard). Explore the repository first. Then write an implementation plan for this feature request: add an endpoint that exports the calling user's tasks as CSV. Save the plan to PLAN.md in the repository root. The plan is for another engineer who has never seen this codebase and will execute it without asking you questions.
expectations: The plan names the exact existing files this change touches, including taskboard/routes/tasks.py AND a file under tests/ — paths exactly as they exist in this repository, not approximations.
Every implementation step carries its own concrete verification: an exact runnable command or a concrete observable check for that step — not a single generic 'run the tests' at the end.

The work is decomposed into small ordered steps, each naming the specific route, function, or header it adds or changes (e.g. the new endpoint path, the Content-Type/Content-Disposition handling), never vague units.
The plan specifies the automated tests to add by test file and concrete test-function-level description, including at least one CSV-content assertion (columns or escaping) and one authorization case.

A zero-context engineer could execute the plan alone: the plan states how the endpoint authenticates (the existing X-Auth-Token / _auth pattern in taskboard/routes/tasks.py) and which task fields map to which CSV columns.
Scope discipline: the plan introduces no unrequested infrastructure (no pandas or export framework, no async jobs) and explicitly bounds what is out of scope.

task: This directory contains a small Flask task-tracking API (taskboard). Explore the repository first. Then write an implementation plan for this feature request: per-user rate limiting on all task endpoints (a fixed number of requests per time window per token), returning HTTP 429 when exceeded. Save the plan to PLAN.md in the repository root. The plan is for another engineer who has never seen this codebase and will execute it without asking you questions.
expectations: The plan names the exact existing files this change touches, including taskboard/routes/tasks.py or taskboard/app.py AND a file under tests/ — paths exactly as they exist in this repository, not approximations.
Every implementation step carries its own concrete verification: an exact runnable command or a concrete observable check for that step — not a single generic 'run the tests' at the end.

The work is decomposed into small ordered steps, each naming the specific mechanism it changes (e.g. a decorator or before_request hook by name, where the counter state lives, the 429 response shape), never vague units.

The plan specifies the automated tests to add by test file and concrete test-function-level description, including how the tests control or fake time to exercise the window boundary deterministically.

A zero-context engineer could execute the plan alone: the plan states where the rate-limit state is stored and why that choice fits this codebase's single-process JSON-file design (documented in taskboard/storage.py), anchored to file locations.
Scope discipline: the plan introduces no unrequested infrastructure (no Redis, middleware package, or proxy) and explicitly bounds what is out of scope.

task: This directory contains a small Flask task-tracking API (taskboard). Explore the repository first. Then write an implementation plan for this change: migrate storage from the single JSON file to SQLite while keeping every existing HTTP endpoint's observable behavior unchanged. Save the plan to PLAN.md in the repository root. The plan is for another engineer who has never seen this codebase and will execute it without asking you questions.
expectations: The plan names the exact existing files this change touches, including taskboard/storage.py AND both existing test files under tests/ — paths exactly as they exist in this repository, not approximations.
Every implementation step carries its own concrete verification: an exact runnable command or a concrete observable check for that step — not a single generic 'run the tests' at the end.

The work is decomposed into small ordered steps, each naming the specific function or interface it changes (the load/save/next_task_id surface in taskboard/storage.py, the STORAGE_PATH config in taskboard/app.py), never vague units.

The plan preserves the existing storage.py call surface or explicitly enumerates every caller that must change (taskboard/routes/tasks.py, taskboard/routes/users.py), and keeps the existing tests passing as the compatibility gate.

A zero-context engineer could execute the plan alone: the plan gives the SQLite schema (tables and columns mapped from the JSON shape documented in taskboard/storage.py) and states how existing on-disk JSON data is migrated or explicitly declares data migration out of scope.
Scope discipline: the plan introduces no unrequested infrastructure (no ORM, no migration framework beyond stdlib sqlite3) and explicitly bounds what is out of scope.
